coredata.dc:title: Accurate identification of the geographical origins of lily using near-infrared spectroscopy combined with carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin nanocomposite and chemometrics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wanjun
Hu, Zikang
Wei, Liuna
Chen, Hengye
Liu, Tingkai
Wang, Siyu
Guan, Yuting
Yang, Xiaolong
Yang, Jian
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: Near-infrared spectroscopy technique is a prevailing tool for quality control of foods and traditional Chinese medicines. However, it usually faced the problems of severe peak overlap, low classification accuracy and poor specificity. In this work, the potential of carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin nanocomposite-based nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy sensor combined with chemometric method was investigated for the accurate identification lily from different geographical origins. Partial least squares-discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) was used for differentiating geographical origins of lily based on the collected traditional and nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy. Compared with traditional near-infrared spectroscopy, the nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy obtains superior classification performance with 100% accuracy on the training and test set. The results showed that the proposed method based on near-infrared spectroscopy combined with nanocomposites and chemometrics could be considered as a promising tool for rapid discrimination of the authenticity of food and traditional Chinese medicine in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lily
Geographical origins
Carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin
Near-infrared spectroscopy
Chemometrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Identification of the geographical origin of lily based on PLS-DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The original NIR spectra of lily samples from twelve different geographical origins (A) and the nano-effect NIR spectra of lily samples from twelve different geographical origins (B).
The TEM image of CDs (A), TMPP (B) and CDs-TMPP (C).
The changes of fluorescence spectra of CDs-TMPP in the presence of several different active components in lily.

The estimated dummy vectors provided by PLS-DA based on original NIR spectra refer to the variety memberships of the lily samples in the training sets and the test sets (A) The estimated dummy vectors provided by PLS-DA based on nano-effect NIR spectra refer to the variety memberships of the lily samples in the training sets and the test sets (B).
The division results of the training set and prediction set for PLSDA model.
Classification results obtained by PLS-DA method based on the traditional NIR spectra and nano effect NIR spectra.
The sensitivity and specificity provided by PLS-DA method for all classes based on original NIR spectra and nano effect NIR spectra.

head.ce:title: Accurate identification of the geographical origins of lily using near-infrared spectroscopy combined with carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin nanocomposite and chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Dao-di Herbs, National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, Beijing 100700, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Dao-di Herbs
National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica
China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Dao-di Herbs, National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, Beijing 100700, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wanjun Long and Zikang Hu equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Near-infrared spectroscopy technique is a prevailing tool for quality control of foods and traditional Chinese medicines. However, it usually faced the problems of severe peak overlap, low classification accuracy and poor specificity. In this work, the potential of carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin nanocomposite-based nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy sensor combined with chemometric method was investigated for the accurate identification lily from different geographical origins. Partial least squares-discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) was used for differentiating geographical origins of lily based on the collected traditional and nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy. Compared with traditional near-infrared spectroscopy, the nano-effect near-infrared spectroscopy obtains superior classification performance with 100% accuracy on the training and test set. The results showed that the proposed method based on near-infrared spectroscopy combined with nanocomposites and chemometrics could be considered as a promising tool for rapid discrimination of the authenticity of food and traditional Chinese medicine in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Lily, commonly called as “Bai-he”, is a perennial herbaceous bulbous plant in the family Liliaceae, which is rich in polysaccharides, steroidal saponins, phenolic glycerides, phenolic acids, flavonoids and alkaloids. It is native to China and later widely cultivated in East Asia, Europe and North America [1,2]. Lily is usually consumed as food and medicine in China. Lily bulbs have been officially listed in Chinese Pharmacopoeia for their various pharmacological effects, and have long been considered as a good medicine for treatment of palpitations, insomnia and hemoptysis [3,4]. Moreover, lily bulbs are also a popular vegetable in our daily diet due to its high nutritional value [5,6]. In recent years, with the improvement of people’s living standards, the quality of lily has become the main demand of consumers. Generally, the quality and chemical composition of lily is closely related to its geographical origin and growth environment conditions [7]. Hence, the geographic origin of lily has attracted more and more attention from consumers. China is the main producer of lily, and it is widely cultivated in various regions, e.g., Gansu, Hunan and Jiangsu provinces. Among, lily produced in Lanzhou (Gansu province, China), Longhui (Hunan province, China) and Longshan (Hunan province, China) is recognized as high quality, and its protected geographical indication (PGI) has been approved by Chinese authorities [8,9]. Usually, the cost of lily with PGI label is higher than that of other regions [10,11]. As a result, driven by interests, lily is often the target for mislabeling. Some illegal retailers deliberately mislabel low-cost lilies from other geographical origin as lilies with PGI label, which has become an increasing problem in market [12]. More seriously, this wrong description may affect their actual treatment effectiveness.

In order to protect the interests and health of public, many researchers have been focusing on developing effective and reliable methods to determine the geographical origins of lily. Traditional methods of identifying the geographical origin of lily mainly depend on experienced experts based on the size and shape of lily. This type of subjective evaluation requires extensive experience and is easily affected by the external environment. In addition, some chromatography techniques, such as thin-layer chromatography [13], gas chromatography and liquid chromatography [14], have been successfully applied for determining the geographical origins of lily. Atomic absorption spectrometry, mineral element fingerprints [15] and morphological DNA technology [16] have also been proposed for identifying the geographical origins of lily and achieved good results. However, these analytical methods encountered some challenges in practical application. They usually required expensive instruments, professional operation and time-consuming and laborious sample pretreatment procedures. Nowadays, as a common spectroscopic technique, near-infrared spectroscopy has been popular and powerful analytical tool in use for quality control and confirmation of the geographical origins of foodstuffs and traditional Chinese medicines, since it has the advantages of fast speed, high efficiency, strong versatility, ease of measurement and no sample preparation [17–20]. Near-infrared spectroscopy covers the wavelength range of 800–2500 nm and mainly records the spectral bands that correspond to CH, OH and NH vibrations of organic molecules in the samples. However, due to the complex composition of lily, the original near-infrared spectra peaks often overlap severely, and some significant differences of spectral peak cannot be obtained. Therefore, the original near-infrared spectroscopy is difficult to directly use for lily quality evaluation. In order to solve this dilemma, one often resorts to various data preprocessing methods, such as, standard normal variate (SNV) and multiplicative scatter correction (MSC). Even so, sometimes it’s hard to obtain perfect classification results [21]. Hence, it is still a challenging task to propose rapid and cost-effective methods for identifying the geographical origins of lily.

Fortunately, with the rapid development of nanotechnology, the above barriers to near-infrared spectroscopy may be readily solved. The chemical components in the lily interact with nanomaterials through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and steric hindrance, which can greatly amplify or enhance differences in near-infrared spectroscopy peaks, thus providing better classification performance. At present, a large number of sensors based on nanomaterials are widely used to monitor food safety and authenticity due to their low-cost, high sensitivity, and convenience [22–24]. In previous studies, Lv et al. completed the identification of the geographical origin of Dendrobium using near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy sensor combined with porphyrin and chemometrics [25]. In addition, in the previous research of our team, we also developed a GO-TAP-based nano-effect MIR spectroscopy sensor combined with chemometrics to realize the identification of Pu-erh tea varieties and the prediction of age [26]. These studies show that compared with traditional NIR or MIR spectroscopy method, the nano-effect spectroscopy sensor method had better performance with 100% recognition accuracy. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on identifying the geographical origin of lily by combining nanomaterials with near-infrared spectroscopy.

As a very popular nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have many merits, such as remarkable optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, good stability, environmental friendliness, and they have been widely used in chemical sensors [27,28]. Porphyrins possess a rigid macrocyclic structure, a large π-conjugation system, and various functional groups and substituents, enabling it to recognize multifarious molecules [29,30]. By attaching CDs to the porphyrin surface, the specific surface area of the material can be increased, which is conducive to improving its response to the sample. In this work, the feasibility of carbon dot-tetramethoxyporphyrin nanocomposite (CDs-TMPP)-based nano-effect NIR spectroscopy sensor combined with chemometrics was investigated for the accurate identification the geographical origins of lily. Firstly, CDs-TMPP is synthesized by hydrothermal method, and the nano-effect NIR spectra of the lily in the presence of CDs-TMPP were collected. Then, partial least squares-discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) model was used for differentiating geographical origins of lily based on the collected nano-effect NIR spectra. Finally, the classification results of the proposed method were compared with the original near-infrared spectroscopy.

Tetra-(4-methoxy) porphyrin (TMPP) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, (Shanghai, China). 70% methanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), citric acid and ammonia (NH3·H2O) were bought in Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The standards of syringate, ferulic acid, vanillic acid, chlorogenic acid, β-sitosterol, kaempferol, catechin, cumaric acid, and quercetin with mass purity > 98% were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents in the study are of analytical grade. Ultrapure water used in the experiment was prepared by the Molgeneral 220a water purification system.

CDs-TMPP was synthesized by microwave heating method and one-step hydrothermal method. The specific synthesis steps are as follows: 0.1 g citric acid and 1.5 mL ammonia were firstly dissolved in 10 mL water. Then, the solution was heated at high fire (H1) in a microwave oven for 5 min to obtain a yellow transparent carbon dot solution. Tetramethoxy porphyrin provided by our laboratory was dissolved in DMF and mixed with carbon dot solution at a mass ratio of 1:1. Finally, the mixed solution was stirred at room temperature for 10 min, and the powder CDs-TMPP was obtained through freeze-drying method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, USA) was used to characterize CDs-TMPP to prove the successful synthesis of the composite. To explore the specific reaction between CDs-TMPP and chemical components in lily, the changes of fluorescence spectra of CDs-TMPP in the presence of several different active components in lily were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan).

A total of 120 lily samples from twelve different geographic origins in China were collected in this study, and each geographic origin contains 10 samples. Among, lily samples from representative regions, such as Lanzhou (Gansu Province), Qilihe (Gansu Province), Baiyin (Gansu Province), Yixing (Jiangsu Province), Longhui (Hunan Province) and Longshan (Hunan Province) were personally picked from local fields, and the lily samples from Fujian and Jiangxi Province were purchased from the local farmers. All lily samples were collected in autumn and identified according to the Chinese lily manual [31] and Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015) [32], which mainly describes some characteristics of lily samples, including the length, width, shape and color of the bulb. The bulbs of lily samples are 2–5 cm in length and 1–2 cm in width and the surface is yellowish white to brownish yellow. The characteristics of lily samples collected in this study were consistent with those described in the Chinese lily manual and Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015). The specific sample information is shown in Table S1. All obtained lily samples were ground into powder with a high-speed universal pulverizer and subsequently filtered through a 200-mesh sieve to obtain homogenous powder. All prepared lily powder was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 60℃ for 24 h before analysis.

Each lily sample is mixed with the CDs-TMPP at a ratio of 200:1 (w/w) and dissolved in 70% methanol. Then, the solution was sonicated for 10 min. After complete dissolution, the mixed solution was freeze-dried to obtain powder sample. An Antaris II FT-NIR spectrometer (Termo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) was used to obtain the NIR spectra. The samples were measured from 4000 to 10,000 cm−1 with 64 scans at a nominal resolution of 8 cm−1. The spectra of each sample were scanned three times and the average was applied for further analysis.

Partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) has been popular in various fields for many years and is one of the most commonly used classification methods [33,34]. It is based on the classical PLS regression algorithm, which look for latent variables (LVs) with a maximum covariance with response variable Y. The response variable Y
N
×
G (N represents the number of samples and G stands for the number of classes) is known as class variable for PLS-DA method, which is a dummy matrix that employs one-hot encoding to describe the class of each sample. In dummy matrix Y, 1 means that the sample is assigned to a specific class, and 0 means the opposite. However, it is hard for the estimated response value provided by PLS regression model to be perfectly 1 or 0. If the estimated response value for a sample is closer to 1, then the sample is considered to belong to the corresponding class, and closer to 0 represents the opposite. Moreover, it is critical for PLS-DA method to choose the appropriate number of LVs. The best number of LVs was selected based on the optimal correct classification rate of cross-validation procedure.

All data analysis was carried out in MATLAB environment, the PLS-DA algorithm is provided by our laboratory. All the programs and software are available and can be obtained from the authors. In this work, some parameters, including correct classification rate, sensitivity and specificity, were adopted for assessing the classification performance of PLS-DA model. Correct classification rate is defined as (the number of samples correctly classified/the total number of samples) × 100%. Sensitivity is regarded as the ability of the model to correctly recognize samples belonging to this class, which can be obtained by TP/ (TP + FN) (TP is true positive and FN denotes false negatives). Conversely, specificity represents the ability of the model to refuse samples from all other classes, which can be calculated by TN/ (TN + FP) (TN represents true negatives and FP means false positive).

The raw NIR spectra of the lily sample from twelve geographical origin are illustrated in Fig. 1 A. As seen from Fig. 1A, the spectral profiles of lily samples from different geographical sources are very similar in appearance and trend. The characteristic absorption peaks could be summarized as: the absorption peak at 4120–4180 cm−1 originates from to the second-order double-frequency vibration of the carbonyl group. The peak at 4255–4320 cm−1 may be caused by the combination of carbon single bond stretching and single bond deformation. The peak at 4760 cm−1 is related to the combination of CO stretching and OH deformation. The peak at 5160 cm−1 is considered as characteristic absorption bands of the combination of the baseband of OH stretching and the first overtone of CO deformation. The first overtones of CH stretching in various groups can be seen at 5990–7040 cm−1. The peaks located at 8260 cm−1 might be associated with the second overtone of CH and the first overtones of OH and NH stretching modes. These groups may be related to catechins, epicatechins, β-sitosterol and amino acids in lily. Obviously, owing to the high similarity and heavy overlap of the spectra, it is still difficult to distinguish the geographical origins of lily sample by traditional NIR spectroscopy. Fig. 1B exhibited the nano-effect NIR spectra of lily samples from twelve geographical origin in the presence of CDs-TMPP. As can be seen from Fig. 1B, compared with the original NIR spectra, the position and shape of the characteristic peak of lily remains basically unchanged. However, the intensity of the NIR spectra is obviously weakened in the range of 4000–7000 cm−1, and slightly increased in the range of 7000–10000 cm−1. Besides, the overall band absorption peaks of the nano-effect NIR spectra from 7000 cm−1 to 10000 cm−1 are more dispersed than those of the original NIR spectra. Moreover, we have calculated the paired t-test at the characteristic peak by using the SPSS software to evaluate whether the sample spectra of Fig. 1A and the spectra of Fig. 1B have significant differences, and the results are shown in Table S2. All the t-test values are greater than the critical value (
t
0.05
9
= 2.3) at a 95% confidence interval. Hence, there are significant differences between the sample spectra of Fig. 1A and the spectra of Fig. 1B. In addition, the variance results also show that the differences between the spectra of the sample of Fig. 1B at the above 7220 cm−1 are greater than those of Fig. 1A. Meanwhile, we tried to calculate classification results above 7220 cm−1 based on the traditional NIR spectra and nano-effect NIR, and the results are shown in Table S3. It can be seen that, compared with the original NIR spectra, nano-effect NIR improved the correct classification rate of training set and prediction set. In general, the spectra differences of lily samples from different geographical origin increased in the presence of CDs-TMPP, which provides a basis for classification.

The TEM measurements were used to determine the dispersion states of CDs, TMPP and CDs-TMPP, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 2 . The TEM image (Fig. 2A) of CDs shows that CDs particles were spherical, regular in shape and evenly distributed. While the shape of TMPP is regular sheet (Fig. 2B). In addition, as shown in Fig. 2C, the shape of CDs-TMPP is no longer a regular. Hence, we can speculate that CDs is attached to the surface of TMPP, resulting in uneven edges. Subsequently, we investigated the changes of fluorescence spectra of CD-TMPP in the presence of several different active components in lily, and the results are described in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3, the maximum emission wavelength of CDs-TMPP is 425 nm. When different active components in lily interact with CDs-TMPP, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm changed. Among, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm increased in the presence of ferulic acid, while the fluorescence intensity decreased to varying degrees in the presence of other components. In addition, the FT-IR analysis was used to investigate the surface functional groups of CDs-TMPP, as shown in Fig. S1, the characteristic absorption band at 2400–3400 cm−1 derived from the stretching vibration of OH, and the band at 1700 cm−1 was belonged to the stretching vibration of CO, which indicated the surface functional groups of CDs-TMPP is COOH. In the presence of different active components in lily, the frequency and wavenumber of OH stretching vibration in CDs-TMPP are changed. This may be because the active ingredients (such as flavonoids) in lily samples have the group of OH, which may interact with CD-TMPP by hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the active components in lily may interact with CDs-TMPP through hydrogen bonding to amplify or enhance the differences in NIR spectra peaks. Moreover, the content of these active ingredients may vary depending on the geographic origin of lily. All these results indicated that CDs-TMPP nanomaterials can amplify the differences of near-infrared spectral peaks of lily samples from different geographic origins, which is also confirmed by nano-effect near-infrared spectra (see Fig. 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the geographical origin of lily based on PLS-DA
ce:section.ce:para: PLS-DA was used to establish discriminant model for identifying the geographic origins of lily based on the collected original and nano-effect NIR spectra data. In order to construct PLS-DA model, random sampling algorithm was used for the selection of training set samples and prediction set samples, and the results are shown in Table 1 . Moreover, the complexity parameters of the PLS-DA model, namely the optimal LVs, were chosen according to the optimal correct classification rate of six-fold cross-validation procedure based on training set. The results showed the optimal number of LVs is 4. Subsequently, PLS-DA model were used to validate the training set and predict the test set, and the classification results are shown in Table 2 . The estimated dummy vectors of the training set and test set provided by PLS-DA are displayed in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Table 2 that the original NIR spectroscopy combined with PLS-DA method for the correct classification rate of the training set and prediction set were 91.7% and 89.6%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4A, in the training samples, 5 lily samples (68th, 69th, 70th, 71th, 72th) from Baiyin (Gansu province, China) were all incorrectly classified as Qilihe (Gansu province, China). Similarly, in the test sample, there are also 5 samples of misjudgment. The second test sample from Lanzhou was misclassified as Jiangsu. Three test samples (44th, 46th, 47th) from Baiyin (Gansu province, China) were incorrectly classified as Qilihe (Gansu province, China). One test sample (45th) from Baiyin (Gansu province, China) was incorrectly classified as Lanzhou (Gansu province, China). This may be due to the fact that most of the misclassified samples came from different places in Gansu province, their geographical location is relatively close, and the composition of lilies is very similar. After the addition of CDs-TMPP, the nano-effect NIR sensor combined with the PLS-DA method was used to identify the same batch of lily samples. The classification results are displayed in Table 2 and Fig. 4B, as can be seen from Table 2 and Fig. 4B, the correct classification rate for training set and prediction set is 100.00% based on nano-effect NIR sensor combined with the PLS-DA method. This is because the CDs-TMPP interacts with the components of lily to amplify the difference between lily samples from different geographic origins, so that the subtle differences between lily samples can be accurately screened by the PLS-DA model.

In addition, common performance parameters, such as sensitivity and specificity, was also adopted for evaluating the performances of the analytical method, and the results are collected in Table 3 . The sensitivity and specificity for all classes based on original NIR spectroscopy combined with PLS-DA method were ranged from 20.0% to 100.0% and 93.00% to 100.0%, respectively. For nano-effect near-infrared sensor combined with PLS-DA method, the sensitivity and specificity for all classes were both 100.0%. By comparing the classification results of the two methods, one can see that the nano-effect near-infrared sensor combined with PLS-DA method shows better classification performance, which is reflected in the 100.0% correct classification rate for training and test set and excellent sensitivity and specificity. These results show that the proposed CDs-TMPP-based nano-effect near-infrared sensor combined with PLS-DA method can be a powerful tool for the rapid distinguishing the geographical origin of lily.

In this work, CDs-TMPP-based nano-effect NIR spectroscopy sensor combined with PLS-DA method is proven to be useful for simply, rapidly and accurately distinguishing the geographical origin of lily. Compared with traditional NIR spectroscopy, the nano-effect NIR spectroscopy can obtain distinctive feature information, which greatly improves the accuracy of model identification. The nano-effect NIR spectroscopy shows good classification results with 100% CCR on the training and test set, which is superior to the traditional NIR spectroscopy. This may be due to the interaction between CDs-TMPP and the chemical constituents of lily, which increases the specificity of NIR spectroscopy. The above results strongly support that the proposed CDs-TMPP-based nano-effect NIR spectroscopy sensor combined with PLS-DA method has a promising potential in rapid classification and authenticity identification of foods and traditional Chinese medicine.

Wanjun Long: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Validation. Zikang Hu: Data curation, Visualization. Liuna Wei: Visualization. Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology. Tingkai Liu: Investigation. Siyu Wang: Investigation. Yuting Guan: Investigation. Xiaolong Yang: Supervision. Jian Yang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Haiyan Fu: Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2020YFC1712700) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant Nos. 32122068, 31972164, 21776321, 32001789].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120932.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
